pages.raw_text:
Memories of the free and easy
days in Spokane in the early '9Os
when men tried to steal horses and
go scot free are revived by the
suit brought this morning by
Grant Dyer to clear title to 152
acres of land on the Spokane river
just below Nine Mile bridge. The
situation also illustrates the truth
Of the proportion that the evil that
men do live after them.


In 1892 Tremain H. Fetterly filed
on homestead, now owned and
for the last IA years in open and
notorious possession of a direct
line of possessors, according to the
abstractor. In that year Fetterly
disappeared from public view as
a fugitive from justice. He had
served a term for horse stealing.
He was about due for another
when he took the hike and left
his wife, Martha, to fill out the
homestead conditions. This she
did, and in 1897 a government pat
ent was given her.


In 1898 Grant Dyer bought her
perfected homestead right. Some
years before Fetterly's son, Wal
ter, had figured in conviction for
larceny and the authorities at the
courthouse believe that he was the
remnant of the famous Nine Mile
horse thief gang.


When Mrs. Fetterly perfected
her homestead and the patent was
issued to her it was in the name Of
her husband. He has not been
heard from except a rumor that
he died in Montana under an as
sumed name in 1900. But this can
not be proven and now Mr.. Dyer
Is forced to bring suit to have all
claim that he or his heirs may
have before he can sell his prop
erty.
